Because the input contains a small number of identical coordinates (45 duplicate coordinates across the 1024 points) I applied a tiny deterministic jitter (eps ≈ 1e-6 scaled per index) to break degeneracies while preserving the original layout. The triangulation was computed on those jittered coordinates using a standard Delaunay implementation; triangles are returned as triples of the original point indices (0-based). With the jittered coordinates the triangulation has no crossing edges, no triangle interior contains any (jittered) input point, and the Delaunay property (maximizing the minimum angle) is satisfied. If you need a triangulation strictly using the exact original integer coordinates (without any perturbation), tell me how you want duplicates handled (merge identical points or allow degenerate
